nameMeanings.name: Rishton
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: English:  see  Rushton  . Early bearers: Aleander Rishton, 1560 in   IGI   (Great Harwood, Lancs); Anna Rishton, 1598, Elizabeth Rushton, 1635 in   IGI   (Altham, Lancs); Robert Rishton, 1603, James Rushton, 1627, Robert Riston, 1757 in   IGI   (Colne, Lancs).

variants.name: Rushton
meanings: English: habitational name from Rishton (Lancashire) and Rushton (Staffordshire), and perhaps also from Rushton (Cheshire, Northamptonshire), Rushton in East Stoke (Dorset), and Rhiston (Shropshire). The placenames derive from Old English risc ‘rush’ + tūn ‘farmstead, estate’.

variants.name: IGI
meanings: 1 English: (i) perhaps a variant of Garth with loss of -r- before the final consonant. Compare Thomas Gath, 1600, John Garth, 1619 in IGI (Saint Keyne, Cornwall); Nicholas Gath, 1716, Thomas Garth, 1726 in IGI (Saint Breward, Cornwall). (ii) alternatively, a variant of Goth with unrounding of the vowel, if not vice versa.

variants.name: Riston
meanings: English: habitational name from Long Riston (East Yorkshire), North and South Reston (Lincolnshire), and East and Sco Ruston (Norfolk). The placenames derive from Old English hrīs ‘brushwood’ + tūn ‘farmstead, estate’. The name may sometimes have been confused with Ritson .
Scottish: habitational name from Reston in Coldingham (Berwickshire), cognate with 1 above.

nameMeanings.name: Gilbert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Old French name of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from gisil ‘pledge’ + berht ‘bright, famous’. It was adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to Britain. This was the name of the founder of the only native British religious order (abolished at the Dissolution of the Monasteries), St Gilbert of Sempringham ( ?1083–1189 ), in whose honour it is still sometimes bestowed, especially among Roman Catholics. It gained a wider currency in the 19th century.

historicEvents.title: 100 Years of Conflict Between England and France Ends
historicEvents.description: Treaty of Paris was signed on December 4, 1259, which ended 100 years of conflict between England and France.

historicEvents.title: Jews Expelled From England
historicEvents.description: After years of anti-Semitic behavior, King Edward I expelled Jews from England in 1290.

historicEvents.title: Famine Strikes Europe
historicEvents.description: A famine caused by cold winters and wet summers across Europe caused millions to die due to starvation. Desperation from hunger caused high levels of crime, stampedes in bread lines in London, infanticide, and even cannibalism throughout Europe.

historicEvents.title: First War of Scottish Independence
historicEvents.description: The First War of Scottish Independence began after England invaded Scotland in 1296. The Treaty of Edinburgh was signed in 1328 which declared peace between the two kingdoms and brought the war to an end.

historicEvents.title: The Hundred Years' War
historicEvents.description: The Hundred Years' War between France and England was largely due to the question of legitimate succession to the French crown.

en.lifeSummary: Mrs Gilbert Rishton was born about 1245, in Rishton, Lancashire, England. She married Gilbert de Rishton about 1295, in Great Harwood, Lancashire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 1 son.
